answer:
a. The expected value of a discrete random variable $x$ is defined as $E(x)=\sum x p(x)$, where the summation is over all values of $x$. Then

$$
E(x)=\mu=\Sigma x p(x)=(-5)\left(\frac{1}{9}\right)+(0)\left(\frac{6}{9}\right)+(5)\left(\frac{2}{9}\right)=\frac{5}{9}=.556
$$


The expected value of the discrete random variable $\bar{x}$ is

$$
E(\bar{x})=\Sigma(\bar{x}) p(\bar{x})
$$

summed over all values of $\bar{x}$, or

$$
E(\bar{x})=(-5)\left(\frac{1}{729}\right)+(-3.33)\left(\frac{18}{729}\right)+(-1.67)\left(\frac{114}{729}\right)+\cdots+(5)\left(\frac{8}{729}\right)=.556
$$


Since $E(\bar{x})=\mu, \bar{x}$ is an unbiased estimator of $\mu$.
b. The expected value of the sample median $m$ is

$$
E(m)=\Sigma m p(m)=(-5)\left(\frac{25}{729}\right)+(0)\left(\frac{612}{729}\right)+(5)\left(\frac{92}{729}\right)=\frac{335}{729}=.460
$$


Since the expected value of $m$ is not equal to $\mu(\mu=.556)$, the sample median $m$ is a biased estimator of $\mu$.

meta_info.caption: Biased and Unbiased Estimators-Craps Application
meta_info.section: 5.2 Properties of Sampling Distributions: Unbiasedness and Minimum Variance
